Job now contrasts his former prosperity with his present misery, caused by God withdrawing from him. A gracious person delights in God’s favor, not in the favor of this world. Four things were then very pleasant for a holy person: 1. The confidence he had in Divine protection. 2. The enjoyment he had of Divine favor. 3. The communion he had with the Divine word. 4. The assurance he had of Divine presence. God’s presence with a person in their house, even if it is just a small cottage, makes it a castle and a palace. He also had comfort in his family then. Riches and flourishing families, like a candle, may be quickly extinguished. But when the mind is enlightened by the Holy Spirit, when a person walks in the light of God’s countenance, every outward comfort is doubled, every trouble is diminished, and they may pass cheerfully through life and through death. Yet the sensible comfort of this state is often withdrawn for a season; and commonly this arises from sinful neglect and grieving the Holy Spirit; sometimes it may be a trial of a person's faith and grace. But it is important to examine ourselves, to seek the cause of such a change by fervent prayer, and to increase our watchfulness.

All sorts of people paid respect to Job, not only for the dignity of his position but also for his personal merit, prudence, integrity, and good management. Happy are those who are blessed with gifts like these! They have great opportunities to honor God and do good, but they must be careful against pride. Happy are the people who are blessed with such men; it is a sign of good fortune for them. Here we see what Job valued in his day of prosperity: his usefulness. He valued himself by the restraint he gave to the violence of proud and evil men. Good magistrates must restrain evil-doers and protect the innocent; to do this, they should arm themselves with zeal and resolution. Such men are public blessings and resemble Him who rescues poor sinners from Satan. How many who were ready to perish now bless Him! But who can show forth His praises? May we trust in His mercy and seek to imitate His truth, justice, and love.

Being thus honored and useful, Job had hoped to die in peace and honor, in a good old age. If such an expectation arises from lively faith in God’s providence and promise, it is well; but if from conceit of our own wisdom and dependence on changeable, earthly things, it is poorly grounded and turns to sin. Everyone who has the spirit of wisdom does not have the spirit of government; but Job had both. Yet he had the tenderness of a comforter. He thought upon this with pleasure when he himself was a mourner. Our Lord Jesus is a King who hates iniquity and on whom the blessing of a world ready to perish comes. To Him let us give ear.
